TextENGNew: Even with the windows open, nothing came in but the heat and the drone of the cicadas.
"...Ugh... it's so hot..."
Break time between classes was supposed to be restful, but with heat like this, it was anything but.
Mion and I were both slumped face-down on our desks, wilting.
The younger kids were impressively tough — the heat didn't bother them at all.
They were running laps around the classroom, determined to squeeze every drop of play out of those few minutes.
"...Hey, do schools in the city have air conditioning in their classrooms?"
"Not really... At most, the teachers' lounge gets it..."
"Wow... that's still better than nothing... If I were a student there I'd storm the teachers' lounge and take it over."
``...That's such a Mion thing to say.@k@vS09/01/170100493. ...Ha ha ha... hahhh...``
changeReason: Old says 'You'd be the first student' but Japanese means 'That kind of thinking is so typically Mion.'

TextENGNew: Even laughing felt like too much work in this heat.
...Mion and I gave up on pointless conversation and devoted ourselves to fanning our faces with our notebooks.
My eyes drifted to an empty desk. ...Rena's seat.
Rena hadn't come to school today.
She'd called to say she was sick and couldn't make it.
"...Rena's been off lately, hasn't she..."
"...Yeah. ...Probably just worn down by the heat."
"...Hey, do you really think that's all it is?"
"...Why, you don't?"
``...I think that whole incident has been wearing on her — slowly, quietly...``
changeReason: Original significantly truncated the JPN; new translation restores the full meaning about the incident 'wearing on her slowly and quietly' (JPN じわじわと来てる).

TextENGNew: "Maybe. ...Though the heat piled on top of everything else probably isn't helping — could be summer exhaustion, too."
Having committed murder, dismembered bodies, and been discovered by her friends — the shock of all that must be immeasurable.
We had joined hands at the scene that day, affirming our bond as friends.
Everyone present believed that act of solidarity had settled everything once and for all.
But... could Rena's heart — the one most directly wounded — really find peace from that alone...?
I couldn't stop thinking that she still hadn't found her footing again since that day...
changeReason: Original omitted key detail; new translation restores 'since that day' (JPN あの日から) and the sense of not having found emotional footing.

TextENGNew: "...I figured that if she played with us all and had fun, she'd bounce back eventually."
"...I guess the wound cut deeper than I thought."
"At a time like this — what should I do to cheer her up?"
"...Probably the best thing is to leave her alone, I think."
"Come on... is that really good enough? We're her friends."
"You know how homesickness works? When you're a little lonely and you call home just to hear your mom's voice — apparently that actually makes it worse."
"When you're feeling low, the best thing is to grit your teeth and throw yourself into something — sports, whatever — to take your mind off it."
"...I want her to have that kind of outlet, but... she hasn't been joining us for club activities lately."

``I don't know about Rena specifically, but@k@vS09/03/170300285. people have a craving to lean on others.@k@vS09/03/170300286. When there's someone nearby who supports you and sympathizes with you, you want to depend on them.@k@vS09/03/170300287. ...Haven't you ever felt it? When someone starts comforting you and you just keep wanting more and more?``
"...Hmm... I suppose."

"Leaning on someone endlessly means trying to draw out more and more of their sympathy. And when that happens, even though you're trying to comfort them, the other person can just keep sinking deeper and deeper."
"When you get a taste of being comforted and leaned on — you develop a habit of wallowing in misery."
"...I think Rena understands that about herself, which is why she's deliberately trying to be alone. If all she wanted was comfort and coddling, she could just stay near us and cry."
"So then — how am I supposed to encourage her when she's so low...?"
"From a woman's perspective — it's better to help someone forget their pain than to try to cheer them up."
"Don't comfort her, don't encourage her... What's left? I give up."
"Did you know that 'don't comfort, don't encourage' is a golden rule in psychological counseling?"

"Ultimately, wounds of the heart are designed to be healed by the person themselves. Even if someone else helps, that's not true recovery. So probably all we can do is watch and wait for Rena to find her own way back."
"...Rationally I understand it, but... it just feels cold-blooded. I can't make myself like it..."
I clasped my hands behind my head and stared up at the ceiling, muttering.
Mion gave a small laugh at that.
"As long as Rena has someone as close to her as you to worry about her like that, she'll be fine. She can pull herself back together."
"Just by being close to her? ...That sounds a little too convenient."
"...There is something you can do, Keiichi."
It was Rika-chan.
...I wondered how long she'd been listening to our conversation.
"...There's something I can do?"
``...When Rena wants to speak, or wants to open up about something — being there to quietly listen. That's it.``
changeReason: New translation adds specificity: 'When Rena wants to speak, or wants to open up about something' matches JPN レナが話したいと思った時や打ち明けたいと思った時, whereas original was vague 'when she wants to talk to somebody'.

TextENGNew: "Just listening is enough? I don't need to encourage her?"
"Kei-chan, people feel lighter just from being able to say the words out loud. Sometimes all a person needs is someone who does nothing but listen."
"...Just as Mii said. Being there — somewhere she can come to whenever she wants to talk — I believe that too is precious, as her friend."
What both Mion and Rika-chan said was profoundly true.
I had been planning to clap Rena firmly on the shoulder the next time I saw her and tell her to stop moping.
But thinking about it now, that would have been completely one-sided of me.
It wasn't as if Rena had chosen to feel this way.
She would recover if she possibly could.
If I'd blundered in with careless encouragement, Rena would have felt I didn't understand her pain at all — and she would have been deeply disappointed in me...
"...You're right. ...I wasn't even trying to understand what was in someone's heart. ...I wanted to cheer her up — but that's my desire, not what she needs."
"Thank you, both of you. ...I think I may have had the wrong idea about this until today."
That day... I had convinced myself I'd given Rena a push in the right direction.
...But now I was starting to doubt whether any of it had truly reached her heart.
I might have just dragged her roughly along — thinking her helpless — without caring where she actually wanted to go.
In the truest sense, I had never been on Rena's side...
Just then, Rika-chan's hand gently stroked my head.
"...Keiichi is Rena's greatest ally. You don't need to doubt that."
"Wh — what?! ...Was I thinking out loud again...?!"
"It did reach her heart. You gave Rena something very powerful that day, Kei-chan."
"...Although, come to think of it — you did say some pretty embarrassing things, didn't you? Heh heh heh heh!!"
When we heard the teacher's footsteps in the hallway, everyone scrambled back to their seats at once.
"Sorry for the wait. Class president, if you would."
"All ri-i-i-ise!!"
With Rena absent, club activities were cancelled today.
That was convenient for me, as it happened — I had an errand on the way home.
It wasn't anything particularly significant.
Mom's point card at the village general store had filled up, and she wanted me to exchange it for dish soap.
I thought it was a lot of trouble for just soap, but she explained she was tied up helping Dad with work, so I couldn't argue.
So I turned onto an unfamiliar road to swing by the shopping street on my way home.
That's when I noticed someone peeking out at me from the shadow of a stand of trees up ahead.
...I squinted to see who was playing this little game — and then the figure stepped out.
"Oh? ...Is that Rena?"
Are you feeling better? ...That's what I was about to say, but the words caught in my throat.
Something in Rena's expression was utterly serious.
"...Wh-What's going on?"
"Shh!"
Rena pressed her index finger to her lips, ordering me to be quiet, then scanned the area before urgently signaling me to come over.
I had no idea what was happening, but I did as she said — clamped my mouth shut and hurried over to her.
Rena gestured for me to crouch down and hide in the shadow of the bushes.
I didn't understand why, but there would be time to ask later.
I didn't resist — just followed her instructions.
Rena looked exactly like someone checking whether she was being tailed.
...Mion might pull this kind of thing just to mess with someone — but Rena never plays games like this.
I had no idea what kind of situation I'd found myself in... but I understood the tension, at least — and I made myself as small and still as I could.
"...........All right. ...It's okay now. ...I'm sorry."
Her voice broke the tension out of me.
"...Phew. ...What on earth is going on?"
"I think... someone has been following me lately."
"What...?"
Why would anyone follow Rena — and who?!
changeReason: Meaning expanded: 'Who would follow her...?' to 'Why would anyone follow Rena — and who?!' New translation captures both the 'why' (どうして) and 'who' (誰に) from the JPN.

TextENGNew: The moment I thought it, I realized what a foolish question that was.
"...Don't tell me... the police?!"
Rina Mamiya and Teppei Houjou.
Two people had vanished.
Lowlifes as they were, the police would still investigate when people went missing.
It probably wouldn't take much effort to establish that a woman named Rina had been coming and going from Rena's house.
But... it would be all right.
As long as those bags were never found, everything would be all right.
"I don't know if it's the police or not... but since the night of the Watanagashi, I feel like someone has been watching me."
"What do you mean?!"
As I said that, Rena fixed her gaze directly on me — her eyes completely devoid of emotion.
...Eyes that seemed to be appraising me.
Measuring whether I was worth confiding something very important to.
Rena gripped her own wrist tightly with her other hand, and spoke in a whisper.
"...I could tell you what's happening... but can you keep it absolutely secret?"
"Of course. If you want me to keep quiet, I won't breathe a word to anyone."
"...Good. ...I know I can trust you, Keiichi-kun."
A faint smile crossed her face, and then Rena scanned the surroundings once more before speaking softly.
changeReason: Original omitted that Rena 'spoke softly/in a whisper' (JPN 小声で言った); new translation includes this detail.

TextENGNew: "...They might be searching for Miyo-san's scrapbooks."
"Miyo-san? ...Oh — the nurse, Takano-san? And what scrapbooks?"
"...She said the investigation is being kept confidential, so you probably don't know."
"...Takano Miyo-san was murdered."
"What? ...What?! When?!"
She hushed me sharply. I lowered my voice and let her go on.
"On the night of the Watanagashi, we ran into Miyo-san and Tomitake-san, didn't we? They were attacked immediately afterward."
"...Tomitake-san... was struck by Oyashiro-sama's curse. He clawed his own throat out and died."
"Miyo-san was found burned to death in the mountains of the neighboring prefecture."
"...I-Is that... true...? Nobody told me any of this!"

"Yes. Because the police are conducting a secret investigation. This is the fifth year of the series of mysterious deaths in Hinamizawa. They don't want to cause panic, so they haven't made it public."
"I... I see... I understand why they're keeping the investigation quiet, but... where did you hear all this, Rena?"
"...Keep it secret, all right? ...I've actually been in contact with a detective named Ooishi from the Okinomiya station. He told me."
"Why would he tell you?"
"...I don't really know. He said it was because he felt he could trust me."
Such a sudden, far-fetched story — I was left completely speechless.
...But Rena had no reason to lie.
If it were Mion, she'd be sticking her tongue out at me by now. But not Rena — never Rena.
"I think the reason Miyo-san was killed is probably her research. Did you know she was studying the history and folklore of this village?"
"I'd heard something like that."
Takano-san had a deep interest in the occult and in mysterious things — or so I understood.
I seemed to remember Mion once telling me that she was researching the village's legends of man-eating demons, out of personal fascination.

"...She probably uncovered a very important secret about this village through her research. ...Or perhaps she hadn't actually gotten that close to the truth — but that's apparently not how they read the situation."
"Wait a moment. What secret?"
"...I only borrowed and read a few of her scrapbooks myself. But they were the ones she kept with special care, so I believe very significant things were written in them."
"...So I think I'm probably about eighty percent of the way to where Miyo-san was heading. But... it's so outlandish that... even I can barely believe it."
"...Right now I'm still in the process of verifying whether Miyo-san's theory holds up."
"...So what is it? What secret is so important that knowing it gets you killed...?"
I startled at my own words the moment I said them.
...Which meant that if Rena told me, I might be killed too.
"I don't want to tell you everything just yet. Once I have proof, I will. ...Because it's that outrageous. That unbelievable."
"What do you mean, proof?"

"For instance — I can verify from my own experience that the disease can manifest without any prior knowledge. But there are so many other things I haven't verified yet. Until I work through them, even I can barely bring myself to believe it."
...Not verified?
The way she put it seemed to veer from the heart of the matter, and I wanted to ask her to explain — but I decided not to cut in.
It was sweltering, oppressively humid.
...And yet Rena wrapped her arms around herself as if she were cold.
"Rena. I will always be on your side. ...You can trust me, if no one else."
"...Thank you. ...Those words mean so much to me."
She looked genuinely, deeply glad.
...I could see that she was caught up in something enormous, unable to confide in anyone — and that she had been feeling terribly alone.
...Yes — this is exactly when I need to stand beside her.
"You should keep it secret that you ran into me here today. If they find out we've been in contact, you might be in danger too."
"...O-Okay. ...Isn't there anything I can do to help?!"
"Not right now. But knowing I can ask for your help when the time comes — that alone is an enormous reassurance."
"...I'll lean on you at the end — I promise!"
"Sure. Any time."
"Ssh! A car!"
Rena pushed my head down into the bushes. I didn't resist — just curled up smaller inside the undergrowth.
We held our breath for a while. Then came the sound of an engine, and a white van passed by.
...I was astonished by Rena's sharp hearing.
She'd sensed the car coming while it was still far in the distance.
"Did you see the license plate?"
"Eh? ...No, sorry. ...I didn't catch it."
"I couldn't quite make it out either, but... I think it's the same car as before."
"...Is that the one following you?"
"Probably. ...It passed me several times today along a road that normally sees no traffic. I've been hiding each time to let it go by — but something doesn't feel right."
``Is that them? The ones who killed Takano-san and are after the scrapbooks you have?``
changeReason: Content changed: original mentions 'killed Takano-san and Tomitake-san' but JPN says 鷹野さんたちを殺して (killed Takano-san and the others); new translation omits Tomitake's name and instead mentions 'after the scrapbooks you have', better matching the JPN.

TextENGNew: "Most likely."
...This was truly an extraordinary story — and I couldn't blame Rena herself for struggling to believe it.
Takano-san had uncovered something enormous through her research.
She'd been found out by people who wanted that something kept hidden, and on the night of the Watanagashi they'd killed her — staging it to look like the curse of Oyashiro-sama.
And now those dangerous people were searching for the scrapbooks that held the fruits of her research — scrapbooks that Rena had.
And for that reason... Rena's life was being threatened...?
"Then isn't your house in danger too?!"
"It's fine. The scrapbooks are hidden in my secret place at the dump."
"...I'm worried about Father... but maybe the fact that he truly knows nothing will protect him."
"...Ahaha. You don't have to say anything, Keiichi-kun — it's written all over your face. 'I can't believe any of this because it sounds completely absurd.' Right?"
I wanted to deny it, but felt that a clumsy response would only make things worse — so I answered with a silence that meant yes.

"...That's the normal reaction. I couldn't believe a word of it when I first started reading Miyo-san's scrapbooks either. ...It wasn't until I'd gone through all of them that I began to see a shape to it all."
"...I'm sorry. I do genuinely want to stand by you — that's true. But... I can't see the whole picture, and without that I don't know how to help."
"If I tell you, you might be in danger too."
"You're already in danger, right? Then we're even — don't worry about me!"
``...All right. I'll tell you. ...Miyo-san and the others were probably killed by the Three Families. ...To be more specific — the Sonozaki family. Mii-chan's family.``
changeReason: Name difference: original says 'Three Families' and mentions 'Tomitake' separately; new says 'Miyo-san and the others' matching JPN 三四さんたち (Miyo-san and others).

TextENGNew: "You know about the series of mysterious deaths, don't you?"
"Of course. Every year, one of the village's enemies from the dam conflict ends up dead — that's the pattern, isn't it?"

"The series of mysterious deaths is a kind of settling of scores from the dam conflict. By eliminating the village's traitors one by one, they're trying to bring the dam conflict to a true conclusion..."
"...I can see how it might look that way. Nobody says it openly, but I've heard that people privately suspect the Sonozaki family is behind it."

"But if they simply wanted to punish traitors, they wouldn't need to do it on the night of Watanagashi. There's no reason to kill them at this slow pace — one by the curse, one spirited away, year after year. Don't you think?"
I'd never heard the expression 'spirited away' before, but I understood from context that she meant it ominously — so I said nothing and simply nodded.
changeReason: Key term changed: 'demoning away' to 'spirited away' for 鬼隠し; while both attempt to translate the concept, 'spirited away' is more standard and understandable.

TextENGNew: "In other words, these deaths carry an intention beyond mere punishment — there's a will to restore sacred meaning to the Watanagashi festival. ...To say it more precisely: it's a staged sequence of events aimed at reclaiming the sacred character of Onigafuchi that was lost after the Meiji era."
"Their ultimate goal is to resurrect the kind of primal, absolute faith in Oyashiro-sama that existed in ancient times."
"...The worship of Oyashiro-sama... Are you talking about the Furude Shrine?"
"Yes. Do you think people truly revere the Furude Shrine today? The elders show it a kind of respect — but that's as far as it goes."
"The children treat the shrine grounds as a playground. Most of the villagers see it as a local meeting place. Its sacred character has been lost."
"But the dam conflict became a catalyst for its revival. They saw it as a good opportunity to breathe life back into the worship of Oyashiro-sama."
changeReason: Term changed: 'cult of Oyashiro-sama' to 'worship of Oyashiro-sama'; JPN 信仰 means faith/worship/belief, which is more neutral than 'cult'.

TextENGNew: "...So you're saying that fanatical devotees of Oyashiro-sama are working in the shadows?"

``Exactly. The worship of Oyashiro-sama was managed by the Three Families to begin with. And so the Three Families have passed down a supreme mandate through the generations even to this day: they must restore the worship of Oyashiro-sama.``
changeReason: Term changed: 'cult of Oyashiro-sama' to 'worship of Oyashiro-sama'; also 'supreme mandate' better captures JPN 至上目的 (supreme objective) than the original's phrasing.

TextENGNew: "When you see it that way, you can understand why the Three Families took the lead in uniting the village during the dam conflict — and why they specifically set up the Alliance office at the Furude Shrine, invoking Oyashiro-sama's name when his worship had nearly faded from memory."
"...So the Hinamizawa serial mysterious deaths were the work of the Three Families — no, of a fanatical group devoted to Oyashiro-sama... But wait. Why would they need to kill Takano-san?"

"Because she discovered a crucial secret about this village — one the Three Families have been guarding since the ancient era when it was called Onigafuchi. If that secret were exposed, the sanctity of Oyashiro-sama as a god would be destroyed."
"They learned that Miyo-san's research was getting dangerously close — and so they eliminated her."
"What is this 'crucial secret'?! Even if it puts me in danger — tell me!"
"...I don't want to say yet. Not until I'm a hundred percent certain. ...If I told you right now, I'm sure you would just laugh."
"Come on — do I look like that kind of person to you?!"
"...I just need a little more time. ...The truth is so overwhelming that I haven't fully accepted it myself. Please — just a little longer."
"...All right."
"Don't tell anyone what we talked about today. Not Mii-chan, not Rika-chan — and not Satoko-chan either."
"O-Okay..."
"Then go on ahead. I'll keep watch here a little longer before I leave. You can never be too cautious now."
Rena sank back into the undergrowth and disappeared. I was left alone.
The chorus of cicadas flooded back in an instant, filling the air — as if no one else had ever been there.
...I had no choice but to start walking, though I was still entirely dazed.
In the end Rena hadn't told me the core of it — out of fear of putting me in harm's way.

But what she had said clearly was this: there exists in this village a sinister will bent on reviving the worship of Oyashiro-sama, and it is rooted in the Three Families who hold the village's power at its center.
changeReason: Term changed: 'cult of Oyashiro-sama' to 'worship of Oyashiro-sama'; JPN 信仰 means faith/worship, a more neutral term than 'cult'.

TextENGNew: And those people had orchestrated the series of mysterious deaths toward that end... and killed Takano-san for getting too close to the secret they wanted hidden.
Rena had met Takano-san and, by chance, found herself in possession of the scrapbooks in which Takano-san had recorded her research.
...And written in those scrapbooks was a secret so extraordinary that even Rena, who had read them, could barely bring herself to believe it...
Come on... I'm sorry, Rena, but... you can't be serious, right...?
This isn't a manga serial... a secret occult society working in the shadows, spinning some vast conspiracy... however you dress it up, it's... not funny even as a joke.
...But, well — Rena isn't wrong about one thing.
Even granting it's true... it's hard to swallow.
I could understand now why Rena had held back, saying it was too soon to reveal the truth.
Maybe Takano-san had just led her along...
But... Takano-san really was murdered. That much seemed to be a fact.
...And was it also a fact that someone was prowling around Rena, looking for those scrapbooks...?
Come to think of it, Rena mentioned something about being watched by a white van.
White vans are hardly rare...
Business vehicles — there must be plenty of them.
...Somehow I felt that Rena was simply being hypersensitive right now.
She still hadn't recovered from the shock of that day.
...And so she'd constructed the idea that the police or someone like them was closing in on her...?
Either way, I had listened without interrupting while she spoke.
...That was the right thing to do, I think.
If I'd said something dismissive to her face, Rena would never have trusted me again.
Just then I startled.
A white van was parked at the roadside.
I hadn't seen the plates on the van that passed earlier, so I couldn't be certain it was the same.
But... the number of people inside, and the general atmosphere... those did resemble the earlier van.
There was also something suspicious about it. ...There was no obvious reason to park here.
This was a narrow path through the woods.
Nothing here at all.
No traffic lights, no vending machine, no public telephone.
Under ordinary circumstances you probably wouldn't even notice the strangeness of it.
...But once you'd noticed, it was undeniably odd.
Inside the van were about four men in work uniforms.
...The driver was smoking, but the others were essentially just sitting there, doing nothing.
If they wanted a break or a smoke, there were surely better places for that.
Of course it was possible they'd pulled over in a sudden desperate need for a restroom — but the atmosphere didn't suggest that either.
They lacked the easy looseness that settles over people genuinely at rest.
There was a coiled tension to them, as though they were holding themselves ready for something imminent.
...Could they possibly be lying in wait for Rena...?!
No, surely not — that's impossible.
changeReason: Old truncated to 'No way'; new captures emphatic double-denial まさかまさか and impossibility.

TextENGNew: ...And yet there was no one else on this road.
This was a back path. People rarely came through here, and cars almost never. ...But still, surely... that can't be right...
...I felt terrible thinking it, but... this was probably Rena's paranoia, or something like it.
Delusions born of the mental strain from her friends discovering she'd killed Rina and Teppei.
"Hey, son."
When I passed alongside the van, the voice came without warning.
There was no reason for a stranger to be addressing me.
So I assumed he was speaking to someone else inside the car.
I pretended not to hear and kept walking — and then he called out a second time, this time with a honk of the horn to go with it.
"Hey son, sorry to stop you. Just heading home from school, are you?"
He had the heavy accent peculiar to the village elders. He must be a local, but I didn't know his face.
And I could tell from his not calling me by name that he didn't know me either.
"...Yes, I am."
"You came this way all the way from school, is that right? Turned the corner by the stationery shop and walked along here?"
"That's right. ...Is there something you need?"
They're probably lost, I thought.

...This village has enough odd shortcuts and unfamiliar lanes that you can easily end up somewhere strange if you take a wrong turn. They must have been waiting for someone to pass so they could ask directions.
...That's what I was just starting to think — when he said something that cut right through me.
changeReason: Original significantly altered the meaning; JPN describes the narrator's thought being interrupted (そう思おうとした矢先 = 'just as I was about to think that'), not a direct statement about someone saying something disturbing.

TextENGNew: "Son — didn't you happen to pass a girl on your way?"
"Eh... um... what?"
"A girl in a white hat and white clothes. With a purple ribbon. Did you see her?"
That description could only mean Rena.
"...N-No...! I didn't see anyone..."
I hadn't had any time to prepare myself for that question.
...No — more than being unprepared. I was frightened.
That's why my voice came out so unnaturally high and trembling.
Until moments ago I'd been disbelieving Rena's story. Dismissing it. Quietly laughing it off.
That's exactly why the question hit me completely unguarded.
...My heart was hammering, cold sweat blooming over my whole body.
I'd managed to say 'no' on reflex, but...
...It probably sounded like an obvious lie.
The man in the driver's seat stared intently into my eyes.
He looked as though he were measuring whether I was telling the truth — and my legs started to go weak...
For a while I stood frozen, like a frog fixed in a snake's gaze, unable to move...
But perhaps the same went for those men as well.
They were clearly suspicious characters who had just asked an ordinary villager out of nowhere whether he'd seen a girl. There was no way not to seem strange...
And I think I was right.
The man spoke first, offering excuses before I'd asked for any.
"...Ah, don't go reading anything into it. We'd passed a girl a little ways back, and then here you are instead — so we thought, huh, where'd she go."
"Oh... I see..."
"Just forget about it. Sorry for the trouble, son. ...We'll be heading off now."
As he said that, he started the engine and drove away.
After they left, nothing remained.
Nothing at all.
...There had been no reason for them to park here.
...So then... those men just now... were they really waiting for Rena...?!
I stood rooted to the spot for a while. Gradually my heart settled... and my head cleared.
...Calm down, Keiichi Maebara.
I had just told myself moments ago that Rena's fears were delusions — born of a mind ground down by too many things happening at once.
I turned the man's words over carefully. ...Sure, I'd been startled to be approached out of nowhere by a complete stranger, but he hadn't actually asked anything strange.
Even I — if I'd been walking behind a girl and she'd suddenly been replaced by a boy when I looked again — would have thought, huh, that's odd, even without saying anything aloud.
Come to think of it, the way that man spoke... he had a casual, easygoing manner about him.
So he'd struck up a conversation on a whim to pass the time — and when I'd looked at him strangely, he'd felt a little awkward and left. That's all it was.
Yes... that's all. There's nothing to worry about.
Thinking it through that way, I felt something in me lighten a little — and I started walking home again.
— No. I can't afford to think like that!
changeReason: Old 'This is bad!' captures urgency but misses the Japanese sense of こうしちゃいられない (can't remain idle/must act now). New is closer but both approximate.

TextENGNew: I snapped back to myself and broke into a run toward home. I had to get word to Rena as soon as possible...!!
